user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.35,00,000/- being the Plaintiff's share in a dissolved partnership firm. The Plaintiff and Defendant were partners in M/s ABC Enterprises carrying on business of construction materials. The partnership was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024. Upon dissolution, accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs.35,00,000/- to the Plaintiff as his share. Despite repeated demands, the Defendant has not paid. Plead under Sections 48 and 69 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 and Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. Claim interest at 12% per annum from the date of dissolution. Include cause of action, limitation, valuation and verification. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Suit for recovery of Rs. 35,00,000 representing the Plaintiff's share in a dissolved partnership firm (M/s ABC Enterprises). The partnership was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024, and the Defendant acknowledged the debt but has failed to pay despite demands.

chronology.event: Accounts settled and Defendant acknowledged debt

missing_fields: Plaintiff and Defendant names and addresses
assumptions: Jurisdiction lies at the Defendant's residence or where the firm was situated
The acknowledgment of debt creates a fresh cause of action for limitation

queries: court fee calculation plaint money recovery

4. Suit to abate so far as the deceased plaintiff is concerned. Sub-rule (2) provides that where no such 
application is made the suit shall abate so far as the deceased plaintiff is concerned. Its words so far as the 
deceased plaintiff is concerned mean that the suit shall primarily abate so far as the deceased plaintiff is concerned, 
but they do not mean that the suit shall in no case abate as a whole. If the suit is of such a nature that it can 
proceed in the absence of the legal representative of the deceased plaintiff, it will abate so far only as the deceased 
plaintiff is concerned. A suit by the partners of a firm to recover a partnership debt is a suit of this nature so that if 
one of the partners dies pending the suit and his legal representative is not brought on the record, the suit will abate 
only so far as the deceased partner is concerned. 153 But if it is of such a character that it cannot proceed in the 
absence of the legal representative,

suit was barred by limitation in terms of Art. 58 of the Limitation Act, 1963. The High Court, 
therefore, in our opinion, ex facie committed an error in arriving on the aforementioned finding.56
In a suit filed by a Bank for recovery of money, where the claim of the Bank was more than 10 lakhs, the Civil Court 
has no jurisdiction to entertain the suit in view of the bar created under Recovery of Debts Due to Banks and 
Financial Institutions Act, 1993 and the appropriate remedy was available before the debt recovery Tribunal. Since 
the suit was barred by statute, it was held liable to be rejected under O 7, r 11(d) of the Code.57
In a suit, where permanent injunction was sought prohibiting the defendants from presenting cheques in their favour 
for encashment, it was held by the Delhi High Court that the reliefs sought will amount to restraining the defendant 
from instituting civil or criminal proceedings against the plaintiff on account of non-encashment of cheques which 
would amount to hampering access to justice. The reliefs sought is barred by law being directly hit by the provisions 
of s 41 (b), (d) of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 and as such the injunction can be refused. However, in the light of 
subsequent events of the case, it was further held that the entire suit cannot be dismissed as infructuous.58
In a matrimonial case before the family courts, the Family

converted into a suit for specific performance by way of amendment in a plaint in 1993, 
when the claim for specific performance had become barred by limitation, the amendment cannot be allowed. 41 In 
another case, a gift deed was executed and registered as early as 28 July 1988, which is a notice to everyone 
concerned. Even after filing of the written statement, for three years no steps were taken to file the application for 
amendment of the plaint, thereby causing the right to accrue in favour of the respondent. The application for 
amendment was filed on 11 November 1992, seeking declaration that the gift deed was obtained illegally and 
fraudulently. Such an amendment, if permitted, would defeat right accrued in favour of the defendant, hence the 
amendment was not allowed.42
Where the respondent is in possession of the suit property, a suit for injunction simpliciter without claiming 
possession, is not maintainable. Thus, where an application to amend the plaint to convert it from a simple suit of 
injunction to one for recovery of possession, was filed after nine years of the filing of the suit for injunction, the 
application was held to be hopelessly barred by limitation and was held not maintainable.43

sections.instruction: Draft the suit title line based on the nature of the suit, relief sought, and any monetary amount involved. Use standard Indian court pleading format. If this is a property suit use 'SUIT FOR PARTITION AND SEPARATE POSSESSION' style. If recovery suit use 'SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/-' style. Adapt to the actual suit type from the classified doc_type and intake facts.

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff(s) and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, address. Use competency averment (sui juris / competent to sue). If any detail is missing, use {{PLACEHOLDER}}. Adapt party labels to the suit type (e.g., Petitioner/Respondent for writ petitions).

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction based on the facts. For territorial jurisdiction, state the SPECIFIC factual basis: where the defendant resides, where property is situated, where the cause of action arose, or where the transaction occurred. For suits involving immovable property, jurisdiction is based on where the property is situated (Section 16 CPC). For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the suit valuation and that it falls within the court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Adapt the narrative to the actual nature of the suit: for property suits describe ownership chain and possession history, for recovery suits describe the transaction and default, for family matters describe relationships and events. Reference documents as Annexure labels. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

must_include.description: At least one date or date placeholder

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section citing relevant statutory provisions and legal principles. For EACH provision: (1) State the legal rule it creates, (2) State what conditions trigger it, (3) Show how the facts satisfy those conditions. Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list or those naturally applicable to the suit type. If applicable, include alternative pleas using 'In the alternative and without prejudice...' framing. Include defensive pleading to pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments.

must_include.description: At least one statutory provision cited

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose. Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose (the specific event: default, refusal, denial, dispossession, etc.), (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — subsequent acts that sustain the right (further demands, further refusals), (3) CONTINUITY — why the cause of action is continuing. Anchor to the actual triggering event from the facts, not to procedural dates like notice.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act 1963 (from enrichment or use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation anchored to the cause of action accrual event, (4) that the suit is within time.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section. State the suit valuation and basis (claim amount for money suits, property value for property suits, etc.). State court fee as per applicable State Court Fees Act or use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder. For property suits, state valuation per Section 7 of the Court Fees Act (market value of share) if applicable.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section IF applicable to this suit type. For money recovery suits: pre-suit interest, pendente lite interest (Section 34 CPC), and future interest with rates and justification. For property suits: mesne profits or compensation if applicable, or state that interest is not the primary relief. Adapt to the actual nature of the suit. If interest is not relevant to this suit type, state briefly that the Plaintiff claims mesne profits / compensation as applicable, or omit interest entirely and instead address any financial relief that fits.

must_include.description: Some form of financial relief or interest stated

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must be specific and tied to the relief sought. Adapt prayers to the actual suit type: for partition suits seek preliminary decree declaring share + final decree for division + separate possession + commissioner appointment; for recovery suits seek decree for principal amount + interest + costs; for injunction suits seek permanent injunction + damages. Always include a general relief clause 'any other relief the Court deems fit'.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon. Map to annexure labels. Adapt documents to the suit type: for property suits include title deeds, genealogy, revenue records, survey sketches; for recovery suits include agreement, payment proof, notice; for family suits include marriage certificate, birth records. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents for this suit type with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to be sued. The Plaintiff and Defendant were partners in M/s ABC Enterprises, a partnership firm carrying on the business of construction materials. The partnership was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024. Upon dissolution, accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff as his share. Despite repeated demands, the Defendant has not paid.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text:
JURISDICTION

Territorial jurisdiction: This Honourable District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}} has territorial jurisdiction to entertain this suit for recovery of money arising from the dissolution of the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises. The cause of action arose on 01.06.2024 at the business premises of the partnership located at {{BUSINESS_PLACE}}, where the partnership was dissolved by mutual consent and accounts were settled; on that date the Defendant acknowledged the Plaintiff’s claim to Rs. 35,00,000 as his share, and the Plaintiff offered or demanded payment thereafter. The Defendant resides at {{DEFENDANT_RESIDENCE}} and conducts business at {{DEFENDANT_BUSINESS_LOCATION}}; the Plaintiff’s cause of action thus arose in the territorial jurisdiction of the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}} because the relevant transaction and settlement occurred at the partnership’s place of business and the debt crystallized thereon. This Court is thus competent to entertain the suit under the applicable territorial jurisdiction principles.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The suit is valued at Rs. 35,00,000 as the principal amount claimed, with interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024 until realization, making the total value to be realized Rs. 35,00,000 plus interest. The claim falls within the monetary limits of the District Court exercising jurisdiction in {{STATE}}. The suit therefore lies within this Court’s pecuniary jurisdiction for recovery actions arising from dissolution settlements of partnerships.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. Plaintiff and Defendant were partners in M/s ABC Enterprises, a partnership carrying on the business of construction materials, at the partnership’s principal place of business located at place_names. The arrangement between Plaintiff and Defendant was governed by the partnership deed evidenced by Annexure A, which records the sharing of profits and losses and the rights and duties of the partners. Annexure A.

2. On 01.06.2024, the partnership was dissolved by mutual consent at the partnership premises at place_names. On the same date, the accounts of the firm were taken; the balance sheet reflected that Plaintiff was entitled to share in the net assets; the Defendant acknowledged in writing that he owed the Plaintiff Rs 35,00,000 as his share of the dissolution. This acknowledgment is recorded in Annexure B. Annexure B.

3. After dissolution, the Plaintiff caused to be served written demand notices on the Defendant at the partnership premises requesting payment of the admitted sum; the first such notice was served on {{DEMAND_DATE}} and further reminders were sent thereafter. The notices and correspondence are contained in Annexure C. Annexure C.

4. The Defendant did not make any payment toward the admitted sum, despite the demands; as of the date of filing, no amount has been paid, and the books and records maintained by the firm reflect the outstanding amount due to Plaintiff. Annexure D contains the ledger and related communications. Annexure D.

5. The cause of action arose on 01.06.2024 when the dissolution occurred and the debt became due to Plaintiff; the present suit to recover the admitted share is within the period of limitation prescribed for such claims; the Plaintiff claims a principal amount of Rs 35,00,000 along with interest at the rate of 12% per annum from 01.06.2024; the computation of interest is set out in Annexure E; the plaint is verified by Plaintiff as per Annexure F. Annexures E and F.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, sues the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, for recovery of Rs. 35,00,000 being the Plaintiff’s share in the dissolved partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises which carried on business of construction materials at {{LOCATION}}. The partnership was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024, and on that very date, in the presence of the parties at the firm premises, accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff; despite this acknowledgement, the Defendant has not paid the amount, and repeated demands have been made thereafter from 01.06.2024 onward. The legal basis for the relief sought rests on the following statutory provisions and legal principles. First, Section 69 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 creates a fiduciary duty among partners to account to one another on dissolution and to recover the amounts due from one partner to another as determined in the dissolution accounts; where dissolution is by mutual consent and accounts are settled, any balance payable to a retiring or continuing partner becomes a debt enforceable in a court of law. In the present case, the partnership M/s ABC Enterprises was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024, the accounts were settled on that date, and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff, thereby giving rise to a legally enforceable debt that remains unpaid. The Defendant’s failure to discharge this admitted liability constitutes a breach entitling the Plaintiff to sue for the recovery of the admitted amount with interest; Annexure A contains the dissolution/settlement record and Annexure B contains the accounts reflecting the balance due to the Plaintiff, while Annexure C and Annexure D record the demand letters and the Defendant’s acknowledgment respectively. Second, Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 recognizes that contracts arising from the dissolution of a partnership and the resulting obligation to repay a debt are enforceable in law; the Defendant’s acknowledgement on 01.06.2024 constitutes a binding debtor–creditor contract evidenced by the accounting settlement, and the Plaintiff’s claim rests on that contractual obligation to pay the debt amount of Rs. 35,00,000; the acknowledgment is reflected in Annexure D and the accounting ledger in Annexure B, with the demand letters in Annexure C reinforcing the obligation. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if any defence seeks to treat the claim as outside the realm of a contract or partnership accounting, the apparent contract nature of the obligation is pleaded to ensure enforceability under the relevant contract principles. Third, Article 2 of the Limitation Act, 1963 governs suits upon accounts and provides a three-year limitation period; the cause of action for the admitted and undisputed debt arose on 01.06.2024 when the accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged the liability, and the Plaintiff has instituted this suit within the three-year period; this position is supported by Annexure B (account settlement) and Annexure D (acknowledgment). In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff contends that the acknowledgment dated 01.06.2024 tolls and preserves the limitation for the full amount in respect of the disputed balance, ensuring the suit remains within time. The Plaintiff also pleads that interest at 12% per annum be awarded on the admitted principal from 01.06.2024 until realisation, which is reflected in the contract and account records; the applicable rate is stated in the pleadings and the demand letters (Annexure C) set forth the intent to charge interest. The relief sought includes admission of the debt, a decree for Rs. 35,00,000 with 12% p.a. interest, costs, and such further or other relief as the Court may deem fit. The facts stated herein are supported by Annexure A (dissolution agreement), Annexure B (accounts reflecting the balance due), Annexure C (demands), and Annexure D (Defendant’s acknowledgment), all of which are part of the record in this case. The Plaintiff has prepared this pleading to address likely defenses preemptively; the anticipated defenses such as a claim of settlement, limitation, or lack of privity are addressed by the statutory framework and the documentary evidence, including the dissolution record, the settled accounts, and the Defendant’s explicit acknowledgment of debt. The Plaintiff respectfully prays for a judgment in its favor for Rs. 35,00,000 with interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024, and for such other relief as the Court deems fit. This pleading is prepared for filing before the District Court having jurisdiction over the matter. The factual assertions herein are supported by the documentary annexures and the statutory framework referenced above. Annexures: A (Dissolution record), B (Accounts reflecting balance due), C (Demand letters), D (Acknowledgment by Defendant). 

All factual assertions herein are based on and supported by the documents identified in the Annexures and the agreed facts of dissolution and settlement of accounts on 01.06.2024, and the Defendant’s failure to discharge the admitted liability.

filled_sections.text: The origin of the cause of action lies in the dissolution of the partnership on 01.06.2024, when the accounts of the firm were settled and the Defendant acknowledged that the Plaintiff was entitled to Rs. 3,500,000 as his share, thereby creating an enforceable debt in favour of the Plaintiff against the Defendant for value of the Plaintiff’s capital and his remaining share in the business of construction materials. The accrual of the claim occurred on 01.06.2024, the date of dissolution and admission, and it has continued since then because the Defendant has refused to discharge the admitted debt despite repeated demands, leaving the principal sum unpaid and bearing interest at 12% per annum; the Plaintiff remains entitled to recover the admitted amount along with accrued interest from that date. The cause of action is continuing because the debt remains unpaid, no payment has been received, interest continues to accrue, and the Plaintiff has repeatedly sought payment without success, ensuring that the Plaintiff’s right to sue remains alive and enforceable before the District Court for the principal sum of Rs. 3,500,000 together with interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024 until realization.

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: Limitation: The suit is governed by the Limitation Act, 1963. The applicable Article for the present claim is Article {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} of the Limitation Act, 1963. The period prescribed by that Article is three years. The accrual of the cause of action occurs on 01.06.2024, when the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises was dissolved by mutual consent, the accounts were settled, and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff, thereby making the debt due and payable. The suit is within time because it is brought within three years from the accrual date of 01.06.2024.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff seeks payment of interest on the principal sum of Rs. 35,00,000/-, which became due on dissolution of the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises on 01.06.2024. The Defendant acknowledged on dissolution that the Plaintiff was entitled to his share of Rs. 35,00,000/-, but despite repeated demands the amount remains unpaid. The Plaintiff therefore seeks interest for three periods at 12% per annum, justified as compensating the time value of money and the breach of obligation arising from the dissolution and settlement of accounts.

Pre-suit interest: From 01.06.2024, being the date of dissolution, the Plaintiff claims simple interest at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/-, up to the date of filing of the suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}. The legal basis for such pre-suit interest lies in the general principle that where money becomes due and remains unpaid after the debt accrues, the claimant is entitled to compensation for the use of that money from the date it became due until suit. The claim is supported by the dissolution and settlement of accounts in which the Defendant acknowledged the liability (Annexure A) and by the Defendant’s failure to discharge the liability despite demands (Annexure B).

Pendente lite interest: From the date of institution of the suit ({{FILING_DATE}}) until the date of decree or judgment ({{DECREE_DATE}}), the Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on Rs. 35,00,000/− at 12% per annum. This relief is sought under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, which authorizes the award of interest on money claimed in suits where the amount is due and payable and the defendant withholds payment during the pendency of the suit. The basis is the same: the upon-dissolution liability remains unpaid during litigation, causing a loss of use of funds and requiring compensation to be awarded at a commercially reasonable rate, here proposed at 12% per annum (Annexure A and Annexure B reflect the underlying liability and non-payment).

Future interest: From the date of decree ({{DECREE_DATE}}) until actual realization or payment, the Plaintiff seeks future interest on Rs. 35,00,000/− at 12% per annum. The legal basis for post-decree interest is grounded in the contractual and fiduciary obligation arising from the dissolution and settlement of the partnership, and under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 as applicable to the lawful compensation for the continued withholding of money due under a settled account. Annexures: The underlying facts and the basis for interest are supported by Annexure A (Dissolution and Accounts Settlement reflecting Rs. 35,00,000/− due to the Plaintiff), Annexure B (Demand communications evidencing repeated demands), and Annexure C (Correspondence reflecting the Defendant’s acknowledgment of liability).

IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

IN THE DISTRICT COURT AT {{DISTRICT_NAME}}
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. 35,00,000/- UNDER SECTIONS 48 AND 69 OF THE INDIAN PARTNERSHIP ACT, 1932 AND SECTION 65 OF THE INDIAN CONTRACT ACT, 1872
Plaintiff: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}
Defendant: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}


BETWEEN

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to be sued. The Plaintiff and Defendant were partners in M/s ABC Enterprises, a partnership firm carrying on the business of construction materials. The partnership was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024. Upon dissolution, accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff as his share. Despite repeated demands, the Defendant has not paid.


JURISDICTION

JURISDICTION

Territorial jurisdiction: This Honourable District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}} has territorial jurisdiction to entertain this suit for recovery of money arising from the dissolution of the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises. The cause of action arose on 01.06.2024 at the business premises of the partnership located at {{BUSINESS_PLACE}}, where the partnership was dissolved by mutual consent and accounts were settled; on that date the Defendant acknowledged the Plaintiff’s claim to Rs. 35,00,000 as his share, and the Plaintiff offered or demanded payment thereafter. The Defendant resides at {{DEFENDANT_RESIDENCE}} and conducts business at {{DEFENDANT_BUSINESS_LOCATION}}; the Plaintiff’s cause of action thus arose in the territorial jurisdiction of the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}} because the relevant transaction and settlement occurred at the partnership’s place of business and the debt crystallized thereon. This Court is thus competent to entertain the suit under the applicable territorial jurisdiction principles.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The suit is valued at Rs. 35,00,000 as the principal amount claimed, with interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024 until realization, making the total value to be realized Rs. 35,00,000 plus interest. The claim falls within the monetary limits of the District Court exercising jurisdiction in {{STATE}}. The suit therefore lies within this Court’s pecuniary jurisdiction for recovery actions arising from dissolution settlements of partnerships.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. Plaintiff and Defendant were partners in M/s ABC Enterprises, a partnership carrying on the business of construction materials, at the partnership’s principal place of business located at place_names. The arrangement between Plaintiff and Defendant was governed by the partnership deed evidenced by Annexure A, which records the sharing of profits and losses and the rights and duties of the partners. Annexure A.

2. On 01.06.2024, the partnership was dissolved by mutual consent at the partnership premises at place_names. On the same date, the accounts of the firm were taken; the balance sheet reflected that Plaintiff was entitled to share in the net assets; the Defendant acknowledged in writing that he owed the Plaintiff Rs 35,00,000 as his share of the dissolution. This acknowledgment is recorded in Annexure B. Annexure B.

3. After dissolution, the Plaintiff caused to be served written demand notices on the Defendant at the partnership premises requesting payment of the admitted sum; the first such notice was served on {{DEMAND_DATE}} and further reminders were sent thereafter. The notices and correspondence are contained in Annexure C. Annexure C.

4. The Defendant did not make any payment toward the admitted sum, despite the demands; as of the date of filing, no amount has been paid, and the books and records maintained by the firm reflect the outstanding amount due to Plaintiff. Annexure D contains the ledger and related communications. Annexure D.

5. The cause of action arose on 01.06.2024 when the dissolution occurred and the debt became due to Plaintiff; the present suit to recover the admitted share is within the period of limitation prescribed for such claims; the Plaintiff claims a principal amount of Rs 35,00,000 along with interest at the rate of 12% per annum from 01.06.2024; the computation of interest is set out in Annexure E; the plaint is verified by Plaintiff as per Annexure F. LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, sues the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, for recovery of Rs. 35,00,000 being the Plaintiff’s share in the dissolved partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises which carried on business of construction materials at {{LOCATION}}. The partnership was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024, and on that very date, in the presence of the parties at the firm premises, accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff; despite this acknowledgement, the Defendant has not paid the amount, and repeated demands have been made thereafter from 01.06.2024 onward. The legal basis for the relief sought rests on the following statutory provisions and legal principles. First, Section 69 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 creates a fiduciary duty among partners to account to one another on dissolution and to recover the amounts due from one partner to another as determined in the dissolution accounts; where dissolution is by mutual consent and accounts are settled, any balance payable to a retiring or continuing partner becomes a debt enforceable in a court of law. In the present case, the partnership M/s ABC Enterprises was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024, the accounts were settled on that date, and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff, thereby giving rise to a legally enforceable debt that remains unpaid. The Defendant’s failure to discharge this admitted liability constitutes a breach entitling the Plaintiff to sue for the recovery of the admitted amount with interest; Annexure A contains the dissolution/settlement record and Annexure B contains the accounts reflecting the balance due to the Plaintiff, while Annexure C and Annexure D record the demand letters and the Defendant’s acknowledgment respectively. Second, Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 recognizes that contracts arising from the dissolution of a partnership and the resulting obligation to repay a debt are enforceable in law; the Defendant’s acknowledgement on 01.06.2024 constitutes a binding debtor–creditor contract evidenced by the accounting settlement, and the Plaintiff’s claim rests on that contractual obligation to pay the debt amount of Rs. 35,00,000; the acknowledgment is reflected in Annexure D and the accounting ledger in Annexure B, with the demand letters in Annexure C reinforcing the obligation. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if any defence seeks to treat the claim as outside the realm of a contract or partnership accounting, the apparent contract nature of the obligation is pleaded to ensure enforceability under the relevant contract principles. Third, Article 2 of the Limitation Act, 1963 governs suits upon accounts and provides a three-year limitation period; the cause of action for the admitted and undisputed debt arose on 01.06.2024 when the accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged the liability, and the Plaintiff has instituted this suit within the three-year period; this position is supported by Annexure B (account settlement) and Annexure D (acknowledgment). In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff contends that the acknowledgment dated 01.06.2024 tolls and preserves the limitation for the full amount in respect of the disputed balance, ensuring the suit remains within time. The Plaintiff also pleads that interest at 12% per annum be awarded on the admitted principal from 01.06.2024 until realisation, which is reflected in the contract and account records; the applicable rate is stated in the pleadings and the demand letters (Annexure C) set forth the intent to charge interest. The relief sought includes admission of the debt, a decree for Rs. 35,00,000 with 12% p.a. interest, costs, and such further or other relief as the Court may deem fit. The facts stated herein are supported by Annexure A (dissolution agreement), Annexure B (accounts reflecting the balance due), Annexure C (demands), and Annexure D (Defendant’s acknowledgment), all of which are part of the record in this case. The Plaintiff has prepared this pleading to address likely defenses preemptively; the anticipated defenses such as a claim of settlement, limitation, or lack of privity are addressed by the statutory framework and the documentary evidence, including the dissolution record, the settled accounts, and the Defendant’s explicit acknowledgment of debt. The Plaintiff respectfully prays for a judgment in its favor for Rs. 35,00,000 with interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024, and for such other relief as the Court deems fit. This pleading is prepared for filing before the District Court having jurisdiction over the matter. The factual assertions herein are supported by the documentary annexures and the statutory framework referenced above. Annexures: A (Dissolution record), B (Accounts reflecting balance due), C (Demand letters), D (Acknowledgment by Defendant). 

All factual assertions herein are based on and supported by the documents identified in the Annexures and the agreed facts of dissolution and settlement of accounts on 01.06.2024, and the Defendant’s failure to discharge the admitted liability.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The origin of the cause of action lies in the dissolution of the partnership on 01.06.2024, when the accounts of the firm were settled and the Defendant acknowledged that the Plaintiff was entitled to Rs. 3,500,000 as his share, thereby creating an enforceable debt in favour of the Plaintiff against the Defendant for value of the Plaintiff’s capital and his remaining share in the business of construction materials. The accrual of the claim occurred on 01.06.2024, the date of dissolution and admission, and it has continued since then because the Defendant has refused to discharge the admitted debt despite repeated demands, leaving the principal sum unpaid and bearing interest at 12% per annum; the Plaintiff remains entitled to recover the admitted amount along with accrued interest from that date. The cause of action is continuing because the debt remains unpaid, no payment has been received, interest continues to accrue, and the Plaintiff has repeatedly sought payment without success, ensuring that the Plaintiff’s right to sue remains alive and enforceable before the District Court for the principal sum of Rs. 3,500,000 together with interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024 until realization.


LIMITATION

Limitation: The suit is governed by the Limitation Act, 1963. The applicable Article for the present claim is Article {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} of the Limitation Act, 1963. The period prescribed by that Article is three years. The accrual of the cause of action occurs on 01.06.2024, when the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises was dissolved by mutual consent, the accounts were settled, and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff, thereby making the debt due and payable. The suit is within time because it is brought within three years from the accrual date of 01.06.2024.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

Valuation and Court Fee. The suit is a money claim for Rs. 35,00,000, representing the Plaintiff's share in the dissolved partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises, the partnership having been dissolved on 01.06.2024; accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff, but payment has not been made despite repeated demands. The valuation for court fee purposes is Rs. 35,00,000, the basis being the claim amount for a money suit. The Plaintiff also claims interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024 until realization. Court fee: Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} as per the applicable State Court Fees Act.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff seeks payment of interest on the principal sum of Rs. 35,00,000/-, which became due on dissolution of the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises on 01.06.2024. The Defendant acknowledged on dissolution that the Plaintiff was entitled to his share of Rs. 35,00,000/-, but despite repeated demands the amount remains unpaid. The Plaintiff therefore seeks interest for three periods at 12% per annum, justified as compensating the time value of money and the breach of obligation arising from the dissolution and settlement of accounts.

Pre-suit interest: From 01.06.2024, being the date of dissolution, the Plaintiff claims simple interest at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/-, up to the date of filing of the suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}. The legal basis for such pre-suit interest lies in the general principle that where money becomes due and remains unpaid after the debt accrues, the claimant is entitled to compensation for the use of that money from the date it became due until suit. The claim is supported by the dissolution and settlement of accounts in which the Defendant acknowledged the liability (Annexure A) and by the Defendant’s failure to discharge the liability despite demands (Annexure B).

Pendente lite interest: From the date of institution of the suit ({{FILING_DATE}}) until the date of decree or judgment ({{DECREE_DATE}}), the Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on Rs. 35,00,000/− at 12% per annum. This relief is sought under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, which authorizes the award of interest on money claimed in suits where the amount is due and payable and the defendant withholds payment during the pendency of the suit. The basis is the same: the upon-dissolution liability remains unpaid during litigation, causing a loss of use of funds and requiring compensation to be awarded at a commercially reasonable rate, here proposed at 12% per annum (Annexure A and Annexure B reflect the underlying liability and non-payment).

Future interest: From the date of decree ({{DECREE_DATE}}) until actual realization or payment, the Plaintiff seeks future interest on Rs. 35,00,000/− at 12% per annum. The legal basis for post-decree interest is grounded in the contractual and fiduciary obligation arising from the dissolution and settlement of the partnership, and under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 as applicable to the lawful compensation for the continued withholding of money due under a settled account. The rate of 12% per annum is justified as a reasonable reflection of the time value of money and the loss suffered by the Plaintiff due to the Defendant’s failure to satisfy the admitted liability, with the obligation continuing until realization (Annexure A contains the dissolution liability; Annexure C contains the decree-related relief sought).

In summary, the Plaintiff claims Rs. 35,00,000/− as the principal and seeks interest at 12% per annum for three periods—pre-suit from 01.06.2024 to {{FILING_DATE}}, pendente lite from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{DECREE_DATE}}, and future from {{DECREE_DATE}} to realization—on the basis of the dissolution of the partnership, the Defendant’s acknowledgment of liability, non-payment despite demands, and the applicable statutory and contractual provisions governing money due and withheld in the context of partnership dissolution. Annexures: The underlying facts and the basis for interest are supported by Annexure A (Dissolution and Accounts Settlement reflecting Rs. 35,00,000/− due to the Plaintiff), Annexure B (Demand communications evidencing repeated demands), and Annexure C (Correspondence reflecting the Defendant’s acknowledgment of liability).


PRAYER

PRAYER

a) A decree under Sections 48 and 69 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 and Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, in favor of the Plaintiff against the Defendant for Rs. 35,00,000, being the Plaintiff's share in M/s ABC Enterprises on dissolution of the firm on 01.06.2024, payable by the Defendant to the Plaintiff with interest at the rate of 12% per annum from 01.06.2024 till realisation.

b) Costs of the suit on the ordinary scale.

c) any other relief the Court deems fit.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

9. Annexure D — Written acknowledgment of debt by the Defendant acknowledging liability to the Plaintiff for Rs. 35,00,000, dated {{ACKNOWLEDGMENT_DATE}} and communicated to the Plaintiff at {{ACKNOWLEDGMENT_SERVICE_LOCATION}}.

10. Annexure E — Demand notices/letters issued by the Plaintiff demanding payment of Rs. 35,00,000 from the Defendant, dated {{DEMAND_NOTICE_DATE}} and served at {{NOTICE_SERVICE_LOCATION}}.

11. Annexure F — Ledger extracts and partner’s capital accounts and the partnership bank statements or other financial records reflecting the balance due to the Plaintiff as of the dissolution, prepared by {{PREPARER_NAME}} on {{LEDGER_DATE}}.

12. Annexure G — Copies of correspondence between the Plaintiff and the Defendant relating to settlement, demands, and non-payment, including letters/emails dated {{CORRESPONDENCE_START_DATE}} to {{CORRESPONDENCE_END_DATE}}.


VERIFICATION

First, Section 69 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 creates a fiduciary duty among partners to account to one another on dissolution and to recover the amounts due from one partner to another as determined in the dissolution accounts; where dissolution is by mutual consent and accounts are settled, any balance payable to a retiring or continuing partner becomes a debt enforceable in a court of law. In the present case, the partnership M/s ABC Enterprises was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024, the accounts were settled on that date, and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs. 35,00,000 to the Plaintiff, thereby giving rise to a legally enforceable debt that remains unpaid. The Defendant’s failure to discharge this admitted liability constitutes a breach entitling the Plaintiff to sue for the recovery of the admitted amount with interest; Annexure A contains the dissolution/settlement record and Annexure B contains the accounts reflecting the balance due to the Plaintiff, while Annexure C and Annexure D record the demand letters and the Defendant’s acknowledgment respectively. Second, Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 recognizes that contracts arising from the dissolution of a partnership and the resulting obligation to repay a debt are enforceable in law; the Defendant’s acknowledgement on 01.06.2024 constitutes a binding debtor–creditor contract evidenced by the accounting settlement, and the Plaintiff’s claim rests on that contractual obligation to pay the debt amount of Rs. 35,00,000; the acknowledgment is reflected in Annexure D and the accounting ledger in Annexure B, with the demand letters in Annexure C reinforcing the obligation. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if any defence seeks to treat the claim as outside the realm of a contract or partnership accounting, the apparent contract nature of the obligation is pleaded to ensure enforceability under the relevant contract principles. Third, Article 2 of the Limitation Act, 1963 governs suits upon accounts and provides a three-year limitation period; the cause of action for the admitted and undisputed debt arose on 01.06.2024 when the accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged the liability, and the Plaintiff has instituted this suit within the three-year period; this position is supported by Annexure B (account settlement) and Annexure D (acknowledgment). In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff contends that the acknowledgment dated 01.06.2024 tolls and preserves the limitation for the full amount in respect of the disputed balance, ensuring the suit remains within time. The Plaintiff also pleads that interest at 12% per annum be awarded on the admitted principal from 01.06.2024 until realisation, which is reflected in the contract and account records; the applicable rate is stated in the pleadings and the demand letters (Annexure C) set forth the intent to charge interest. The relief sought includes admission of the debt, a decree for Rs. 35,00,000 with 12% p.a. interest, costs, and such further or other relief as the Court may deem fit. The facts stated herein are supported by Annexure A (dissolution agreement), Annexure B (accounts reflecting the balance due), Annexure C (demands), and Annexure D (Defendant’s acknowledgment), all of which are part of the record in this case. The Plaintiff has prepared this pleading to address likely defenses preemptively; the anticipated defenses such as a claim of settlement, limitation, or lack of privity are addressed by the statutory framework and the documentary evidence, including the dissolution record, the settled accounts, and the Defendant’s explicit acknowledgment of debt. The Plaintiff respectfully prays for a judgment in its favor for Rs. 35,00,000 with interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024, and for such other relief as the Court deems fit. INTEREST

The Plaintiff seeks payment of interest on the principal sum of Rs. 35,00,000/-, which became due on dissolution of the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises on 01.06.2024. The Defendant acknowledged on dissolution that the Plaintiff was entitled to his share of Rs. 35,00,000/-, but despite repeated demands the amount remains unpaid. The Plaintiff therefore seeks interest for three periods at 12% per annum, justified as compensating the time value of money and the breach of obligation arising from the dissolution and settlement of accounts.

Pre-suit interest: From 01.06.2024, being the date of dissolution, the Plaintiff claims simple interest at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/-, up to the date of filing of the suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}. The legal basis for such pre-suit interest lies in the general principle that where money becomes due and remains unpaid after the debt accrues, the claimant is entitled to compensation for the use of that money from the date it became due until suit. The claim is supported by the dissolution and settlement of accounts in which the Defendant acknowledged the liability (Annexure A) and by the Defendant’s failure to discharge the liability despite demands (Annexure B).

Pendente lite interest: From the date of institution of the suit ({{FILING_DATE}}) until the date of decree or judgment ({{DECREE_DATE}}), the Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on Rs. 35,00,000/− at 12% per annum. This relief is sought under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, which authorizes the award of interest on money claimed in suits where the amount is due and payable and the defendant withholds payment during the pendency of the suit. The basis is the same: the upon-dissolution liability remains unpaid during litigation, causing a loss of use of funds and requiring compensation to be awarded at a commercially reasonable rate, here proposed at 12% per annum (Annexure A and Annexure B reflect the underlying liability and non-payment).

Future interest: From the date of decree ({{DECREE_DATE}}) until actual realization or payment, the Plaintiff seeks future interest on Rs. 35,00,000/− at 12% per annum. The legal basis for post-decree interest is grounded in the contractual and fiduciary obligation arising from the dissolution and settlement of the partnership, and under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 as applicable to the lawful compensation for the continued withholding of money due under a settled account. The rate of 12% per annum is justified as a reasonable reflection of the time value of money and the loss suffered by the Plaintiff due to the Defendant’s failure to satisfy the admitted liability, with the obligation continuing until realization (Annexure A contains the dissolution liability; Annexure C contains the decree-related relief sought).

In summary, the Plaintiff claims Rs. 35,00,000/− as the principal and seeks interest at 12% per annum for three periods—pre-suit from 01.06.2024 to {{FILING_DATE}}, pendente lite from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{DECREE_DATE}}, and future from {{DECREE_DATE}} to realization—on the basis of the dissolution of the partnership, the Defendant’s acknowledgment of liability, non-payment despite demands, and the applicable statutory and contractual provisions governing money due and withheld in the context of partnership dissolution. A precise calculation and accounting of the interest shall be ascertained by the Court, and the Plaintiff prays that such interest be incorporated into the decree.

Annexures: The underlying facts and the basis for interest are supported by Annexure A (Dissolution and Accounts Settlement reflecting Rs. 35,00,000/− due to the Plaintiff), Annexure B (Demand communications evidencing repeated demands), and Annexure C (Correspondence reflecting the Defendant’s acknowledgment of liability).
